does: This paper introduces NOHARM, a comprehensive benchmark containing 1,100 primary care and specialist consultation tasks with 12,747 expert annotations evaluating the safety of medical recommendations from 28 large language models. The benchmark spans 10 medical specialties and assesses both omissions and commissions in clinical advice. The study finds that LLM-generated recommendations carried potential for severe harm in up to 22.6% of cases, with errors of omission (missing crucial information or treatments) accounting for over 80% of severe errors. Beyond characterization, the paper conducts a randomized trial with 101 generalist physicians, showing that while AI assistance significantly improved physician performance on medical decisions, physicians often ignored critical advice provided by the models, suggesting misalignment between AI capabilities and actual clinical deployment.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about deploying LLMs in high-stakes medical domains where errors carry serious consequences. It extends prior benchmarking efforts in medical AI evaluation, which have focused mainly on multiple-choice questions and factual recall, by introducing a task-driven assessment grounded in realistic clinical workflows. The paper responds to limitations in existing medical LLM evaluations that primarily measure general knowledge and intelligence without capturing domain-specific safety considerations. It also connects to human-AI collaboration research, investigating how physicians interact with and trust AI recommendations, building on studies of automation bias and appropriate reliance in clinical decision support systems.
enables: This benchmark and findings establish a foundation for developing safer medical LLMs through targeted improvement in areas like avoiding harmful omissions and reducing severe errors at the frontier of model capabilities. The disconnect between general intelligence and safety performance at the frontier suggests that specialized alignment and safety techniques may be needed beyond scaling existing approaches. The work points toward requirements for clinical deployment, including better calibration of AI confidence, improved explanability of recommendations, and human-AI interface design that promotes appropriate reliance rather than either over-trust or under-utilization. Future work can leverage NOHARM to track safety improvements, develop targeted safety interventions, and investigate why advanced models sometimes underperform on critical medical dimensions.
